quiz_question: Who is Erlang Shen's uncle, the supreme ruler of heaven in Chinese mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the Ming-era semi-mythical novel Journey to the West, Erlang Shen is identified as the nephew of the legendary Jade Emperor. He is known as the greatest warrior god of heaven and was a disciple of Yuding Zhenren, who taught him fighting and magical skills including the 72 Earthly Transformations.

quiz_question: From which dynasty's hydraulic engineer Li Bing does Erlang Shen most likely originate?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Erlang Shen's origin is complex but he is most commonly believed to be the deification of Li Erlang, the second son of Li Bing — a hydraulic engineer of the Qin dynasty (221–206 BCE). Li Bing was historically a renowned flood-control engineer in the Chengdu Plain.

quiz_question: In Hindu mythology, who was the first mortal to die and become ruler of the dead?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Yama was considered the first mortal who died and 'espied the way to the celestial abodes'; as a result, he became the ruler of the departed. His role is described in the Upanishads, Ramayana, and Mahabharata.

quiz_question: Who is the divine twin sister of Yama, the Hindu god of death?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Yama is described as the twin of the goddess Yami, and both are children of the sun god Surya (called Vivasvat in earlier traditions) and Sanjna. This divine twin pairing — life/death, sun/shadow — is one of the most striking in Hindu cosmology.

quiz_question: Yama was adopted as king of hell by how many cultures beyond Hinduism?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Yama was adopted by Buddhist, Chinese, Tibetan, Korean, and Japanese mythology as the king of hell — five distinct traditions beyond his original Hindu origins. This makes Yama one of the most cross-culturally shared deities in Asian religions.

quiz_question: Which Vedic god appears more often than any other deity in the Rigveda?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Indra is the most frequently mentioned deity in the Rigveda. He is celebrated for killing the great evil asura Vritra, thereby bringing rain and sunshine to mankind, making him a saviour figure in ancient Vedic religion.

quiz_question: In Hindu cosmology, what kind of being holds the title 'Indra' — is it one eternal god or many?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and the Vishnu Purana, Indra is a title borne by the king of the gods that changes every Manvantara — a cyclic period of time in Hindu cosmology. The current Indra is called Purandhara. This means 'Indra' is more like an office than a person.

quiz_question: How do Buddhist texts treat Indra, king of the Hindu gods, compared to Vedic tradition?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in Buddhist texts Indra rules over the Devas realm within the Samsara doctrine, but is 'a subject of ridicule and reduced to a figurehead status', shown as a god who still suffers rebirth — a radical demotion from his Vedic supremacy.

quiz_question: In how many countries does the mythological Garuda appear in official state insignia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Garuda is part of state insignia of India, Indonesia, and Thailand. Indonesia and Thailand use Garuda as their coat of arms, the Indian Army uses him on their Guards Brigade Regimental Insignia, and the Indian Air Force named their special operations unit after him.

quiz_question: In Hindu mythology, which divine being is described as the eternal enemy of all serpents?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Garuda is 'generally portrayed as a protector with the power to swiftly travel anywhere, ever vigilant and an enemy of every serpent.' He is also described as the king of the birds. His serpent enmity is a recurring theme throughout Hindu texts.

quiz_question: What makes Garuda uniquely related to almost every class of divine being in Hindu mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Garuda 'is also the half-brother of the Devas, Gandharvas, Daityas, Danavas, Nagas, Vanara and Yakshas' — seven different classes of divine and semi-divine beings, all through their shared paternity under the sage Kashyapa. This makes Garuda one of the most cosmically connected figures in Hindu mythology.

quiz_question: For every one cut off, how many did the Lernaean Hydra regenerate?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, later versions of the Hydra story add a regeneration feature: 'for every head chopped off, the Hydra would regrow two heads.' Heracles finally defeated it with the help of his nephew Iolaus by burning the neck stumps after each decapitation.

quiz_question: Besides its regenerating heads, what other deadly traits did the Lernaean Hydra possess?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Hydra 'had poisonous breath and blood so virulent that even its scent was deadly.' This made it extraordinarily dangerous — even approaching the monster risked death. It is why Heracles and Iolaus had to work with such precise coordination.

quiz_question: In ancient Greek speech, what did 'cutting off a hydra' mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in ancient Greece the proverbial expression 'Hydran temnein' (cutting off a hydra) was used to describe tasks that are hopeless or endlessly futile, drawing from the myth of the Hydra's regenerative heads.

quiz_question: Of the three Gorgon sisters, which was the only mortal one?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, of the three Gorgon sisters — Medusa, Euryale, and Stheno — 'only Medusa was mortal.' This is why Perseus could kill her while her immortal sisters survived. Her parents were Phorcys and Ceto.

quiz_question: What happened to Medusa's head after Perseus cut it off?
distractors: It grew into a new body
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Medusa was beheaded by Perseus 'who then used her head, which retained its ability to turn onlookers to stone, as a weapon until he gave it to the goddess Athena to place on her shield.' In classical antiquity, this image appeared on the evil-averting device known as the Gorgoneion.

quiz_question: Which famous winged creature was born from Medusa's blood when she was beheaded?
explanation: According to Wikidata and Greek mythology tradition, Medusa's children include Chrysaor and Pegasus — both born from her blood when she was killed by Perseus. Medusa had conceived them with Poseidon. Pegasus went on to become one of the most iconic creatures in Greek mythology.

quiz_question: How many young Athenians were sent as tribute to the Minotaur every nine years?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'every nine years the people of Athens were compelled by King Minos to choose fourteen young noble citizens (seven men and seven women) to be offered as sacrificial victims to the Minotaur.' This tribute continued until Theseus volunteered and killed the beast.

quiz_question: Who designed the Labyrinth that held the Minotaur of Crete?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Labyrinth was 'an elaborate maze-like construction designed by the architect Daedalus and his son Icarus, upon command of King Minos of Crete.' Daedalus was later imprisoned in his own creation before escaping by building wax wings — the same wings that killed his son Icarus.

quiz_question: What did Ariadne give Theseus to help him escape the Labyrinth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Theseus 'managed to navigate the labyrinth with the help of a thread offered to him by the King's daughter, Ariadne.' He unspooled it as he went in, then followed it back out after killing the Minotaur. The concept of 'Ariadne's thread' survives today as a metaphor for solving complex problems.

quiz_question: In Hindu mythology, who became ruler of the dead by being the first mortal to die?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Vedic tradition held that Yama was the first mortal to die and 'espied the way to the celestial abodes.' This pioneering death qualified him to rule over all departed souls in Naraka.

quiz_question: What does the Sanskrit name 'Yama' literally mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Yama's Sanskrit name literally means 'twin,' referencing his twin sister Yami. He is also known as Kāla (time) and Dharmarāja (king of righteousness).

quiz_question: Which originally Hindu death god was adopted as king of hell by Buddhist, Chinese, Tibetan, Korean, and Japanese traditions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Yama spread from Hindu tradition into Buddhist, Chinese, Tibetan, Korean, and Japanese mythology, all as a ruler of the underworld or king of hell — making him one of the most cross-cultural death deities in world mythology.

quiz_question: Which deity is mentioned most frequently in the Rigveda?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Indra is the most frequently mentioned deity in the Rigveda. He is the king of the devas (gods) and associated with lightning, storms, rain, and war — yet his importance shrank dramatically in later Hindu texts.

quiz_question: Which Hindu storm god is considered the equivalent of Zeus in Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Indra's powers and role are similar to Zeus (Greek), Jupiter (Roman), Odin (Norse), and Thor (Norse) — all are part of the greater Proto-Indo-European mythology, sharing common ancient roots.

quiz_question: In Hindu mythology, what is Garuda's eternal sworn enemy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Garuda is 'ever vigilant and an enemy of every serpent.' The divine eagle and the divine serpents (Nāgas) are cosmological opposites in Hindu and Buddhist mythology — reflecting the real-world predator-prey relationship between eagles and snakes.

quiz_question: What happened when one of the Hydra's heads was cut off?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, later versions of the Hydra story added a regeneration feature: for every head chopped off, two new heads grew back. Heracles had to burn each neck stump with fire (aided by Iolaus) to prevent regrowth.

quiz_question: According to Hesiod, which pair of monstrous parents gave birth to the Hydra?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, citing Hesiod, the Hydra was the offspring of Typhon (the most fearsome monster) and Echidna (half-woman, half-serpent). This monstrous couple also parented Cerberus, the Sphinx, the Nemean Lion, Orthrus, and the Chimera.

quiz_question: Which famous creature was born from Medusa's blood when she was beheaded?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Medusa's children were Chrysaor (a warrior) and Pegasus (the winged horse), fathered by Poseidon. Both sprang from Medusa's blood upon decapitation — giving Pegasus one of mythology's most dramatic birth stories.

quiz_question: How often was Athens forced to send fourteen young nobles into the Minotaur's Labyrinth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, every nine years the people of Athens were compelled by King Minos to send fourteen young noble citizens — seven men and seven women — as sacrificial victims into the Labyrinth. This was retribution for the death of Minos's son Androgeos.

quiz_question: Who gave Theseus the thread that helped him escape the Labyrinth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Minotaur was slain by the hero Theseus, who navigated the labyrinth with help from a thread offered by King Minos's daughter, Ariadne. The thread allowed Theseus to retrace his steps and escape the maze.

quiz_question: Who were the divine parents of Pegasus the winged horse?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus was sired by Poseidon (in his role as horse-god) and foaled by the Gorgon Medusa. Both Pegasus and his brother Chrysaor were born from Medusa's blood when Perseus beheaded her.

quiz_question: Which Greek hero rode Pegasus to defeat the Chimera?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus was captured by the Greek hero Bellerophon near the fountain Peirene, with the help of Athena and Poseidon. Pegasus allowed Bellerophon to ride him to defeat the monster Chimera, which led to many more exploits.

quiz_question: In what enduring astronomical way was the mythological Pegasus honored?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus was long honored as a constellation and is the subject of very rich iconography, especially through ancient Greek pottery as well as paintings and sculptures of the Renaissance.

quiz_question: How many heads did the mythological Cerberus usually have?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cerberus (the hound of Hades) was usually described as having three heads, a serpent for a tail, and snakes protruding from his body. He guarded the gates of the underworld to prevent the dead from leaving.

quiz_question: Which of Heracles' twelve labours involved capturing Cerberus from the underworld?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cerberus is primarily known for his capture by Heracles, which was the last of Heracles' twelve labours. Heracles had to descend into the underworld, wrestle the three-headed dog into submission without using weapons, and bring him back to the surface world.

quiz_question: Which monstrous pair are the parents of both Cerberus and the Hydra?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cerberus's siblings include the Hydra, Orthrus, the Chimera, and the Nemean Lion — all offspring of Typhon and Echidna. Cerberus also has polycephaly (multiple heads) as a medical condition in mythology.

quiz_question: Which ancient sea monster appears in the Book of Job, Psalms, and Isaiah?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Leviathan is a giant sea serpent referenced in the Hebrew Bible — in Psalms, the Book of Job, the Book of Isaiah, and the Book of Enoch. It is often an embodiment of chaos, threatening to eat the damned when their lives are over.

quiz_question: Which deadly sin did Christian theologians associate with the Leviathan?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Christian theologians identified Leviathan with the demon of the deadly sin of envy. This placed the sea monster from the Book of Job into the medieval Christian framework of the Seven Deadly Sins, each governed by a specific demon.

quiz_question: Which older Canaanite sea monster was the direct ancestor of the biblical Leviathan?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Leviathan in the Book of Job is a reflection of the older Canaanite Lotan, a primeval monster defeated by the god Baal Hadad. Scholars have also drawn parallels to the Sumerian sea goddess Tiamat (defeated by Marduk) and Norse Jörmungandr (defeated by Thor).

quiz_question: Which philosopher used the Leviathan as a metaphor for the all-powerful state?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in Hobbes' Leviathan (1651), the mythological beast becomes a metaphor for the omnipotence of the state, which maintains itself by educating children in its favour, generation after generation. This concept of eternal power that 'feeds' on its self-produced citizens remains one of political philosophy's most vivid metaphors.

quiz_question: In which biblical book does the chaos-monster Behemoth first appear?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Behemoth (Hebrew: בְּהֵמוֹת) is a beast from the biblical Book of Job, described as a form of the primeval chaos-monster created by God at the beginning of creation. According to Wikidata, it is also present in the Old Testament and the Bible more broadly.

quiz_question: What does the word 'behemoth' now mean in modern English?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Behemoth's name has metaphorically come to be used for any extremely large or powerful entity. This is a direct borrowing from the Hebrew biblical creature — the word entered English as a general term for anything overwhelmingly massive.

quiz_question: Which pair of chaos-monsters from the Book of Job represents land and sea forces respectively?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, both Behemoth and Leviathan are chaos-monsters appearing in the Book of Job. Biblical scholars have long interpreted them as a paired set — Behemoth ruling the land and Leviathan ruling the sea — representing the full scope of primordial chaos that only God can control.

quiz_question: What type of tree are dryads specifically associated with in original Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a dryad is an oak tree nymph or oak tree spirit in Greek mythology. The word 'drys' (δρῦς) means 'tree', and more specifically 'oak' in Greek. Today the term is often used more broadly to refer to tree nymphs in general.

quiz_question: The Greek 'drys' means both 'tree' and specifically which type of tree?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'drys' (δρῦς) means 'tree', and more specifically 'oak' in Greek. This linguistic duality explains why dryads began as oak-specific spirits but expanded to represent tree nymphs broadly in later literature and modern fantasy.

quiz_question: How many heads did the mythological guard dog Orthrus have?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Orthrus (also Orthus) was, according to the mythographer Apollodorus, a two-headed dog who guarded Geryon's cattle and was killed by Heracles. He was the offspring of the monsters Echidna and Typhon, and the brother of Cerberus — who was also a multi-headed guard dog.

quiz_question: Which multi-headed hound of Hades was Orthrus's sibling?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Orthrus was the sibling of Cerberus — who was also a multi-headed guard dog — as well as of the Hydra. Orthrus himself was the father of the Nemean Lion and the Sphinx. Both Orthrus and Cerberus were offspring of the monsters Echidna and Typhon.

quiz_question: During which lunar phase does a werewolf traditionally transform, according to folklore?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a werewolf in folklore is a mythical being described as a person who shapeshifts into a wolf-like creature during the nights of a full moon. The full moon connection is central to the folklore tradition of the werewolf.

quiz_question: Which Greek monster lived opposite Charybdis in a narrow strait?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Scylla is a legendary man-eating monster that lives on one side of a narrow channel of water, opposite Charybdis. Scylla is first attested in Homer's Odyssey, where Odysseus and his crew encounter her. Later myth adds an origin story: a beautiful nymph transformed into a monster.

quiz_question: How many dog heads did the monster Scylla have according to the Byzantine Suda encyclopedia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Byzantine encyclopedia Suda describes Scylla as having the appearance of a beautiful woman up to the eyes, with six dog heads on each side, and a serpent body below. This composite description reflects the Greek fascination with hybrid monsters.

quiz_question: According to Greek myth, who were the parents of the whirlpool monster Charybdis?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Charybdis's father is Poseidon and her mother is Gaia — making her a child of the sea god and the Earth goddess. According to Wikipedia, Charybdis appears alongside Scylla as a challenge to epic heroes including Odysseus, Jason, and Aeneas.

quiz_question: What Japanese supernatural creature is said to devour nightmares?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, baku are Japanese supernatural beings that devour nightmares. They originate from the Chinese Mo and have a long history in Japanese folklore and art. According to legend, they were created from the spare pieces left over when the gods finished creating all other animals.

quiz_question: What real animal shares its Japanese name with the mythical nightmare-devouring creature?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Japanese term 'baku' has two current meanings: the traditional dream-devouring supernatural creature and the Malayan tapir. The mythical Baku — assembled from divine leftover parts — and the real tapir share the same name in Japanese. The Baku originated from the Chinese Mo.

quiz_question: In Greek myth, who is the personification of Earth and mother of the Titans and Cyclopes?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gaia is the personification of Earth in Greek mythology. She is the mother of Uranus (Sky), with whom she conceived the Titans (themselves parents of many Olympian gods), the Cyclopes, and the Giants. Her Roman equivalent was Terra.

quiz_question: What does the Greek epithet 'Karpophoros' given to Gaia mean?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Gaia's domain was fertility and her epithet was 'Karpophoros'. In Greek, 'karpos' means fruit and 'phoros' means bearing — so Gaia as 'Karpophoros' embodied the Earth as a fertile, fruit-bearing mother. Her Roman equivalent was Terra.

quiz_question: Which Titan ruled during the mythological Golden Age before being overthrown by Zeus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cronus was the leader and youngest of the Titans. He overthrew his father Uranus and ruled during the mythological Golden Age until he was overthrown by his son Zeus and imprisoned in Tartarus.

quiz_question: What annual Athenian harvest festival honoured the Titan Cronus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cronus was likely originally a harvest god, and in many regions of Greece the harvest month was named Cronion after him. In Athens, a festival called Kronia was held on the twelfth day of the Attic month of Hekatombaion to celebrate the harvest.

quiz_question: Which Titan fathered Helios (Sun), Selene (Moon), and Eos (Dawn)?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hyperion was one of the Titans, children of Uranus (Sky) and Gaia (Earth). With his sister/spouse Theia, Hyperion fathered Helios (Sun), Selene (Moon), and Eos (Dawn). Hyperion was, along with his son Helios, a personification of the sun.

quiz_question: According to Hesiod, who was both the son and husband of Gaia in Greek primordial myth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in Greek mythology Uranus (meaning 'sky') is the personification of the sky and one of the primordial deities. According to Hesiod, Uranus was the son and husband of Gaia, with whom he fathered the first generation of Titans. His Latin name is Caelus.

quiz_question: Which Greek sea monster was paired with Scylla in the Strait of Messina?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Charybdis (Ancient Greek: Χάρυβδις) is a sea monster in Greek mythology. Along with Scylla, Charybdis appears as a challenge to epic characters such as Odysseus, Jason, and Aeneas. Ancient sources locate her in the Strait of Messina between Sicily and mainland Italy. Wikidata confirms her parents as Poseidon and Gaia.

quiz_question: In Greek mythology, who is the personification of the sky and father of the first Titans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Uranus (sometimes written Ouranos, Ancient Greek: Οὐρανός, lit. 'sky') is the personification of the sky and one of the Greek primordial deities. According to Hesiod, Uranus was the son and husband of Gaia (Earth), with whom he fathered the first generation of Titans. Wikidata lists his children as including the Titans, Aphrodite, and the Cyclopes.

quiz_question: Which Greek Titaness was the goddess of memory and mother of the nine Muses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mnemosyne (Ancient Greek: Μνημοσύνη) was one of the Titans, daughter of Uranus and Gaia. She was the goddess of memory and the mother of the nine Muses by Zeus. Her name is derived from the Greek word mnēmē ('remembrance, memory'), giving us the English word 'mnemonic'.

quiz_question: In Greek mythology, which abyss served as both a prison for the Titans and a dungeon for the wicked dead?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tartarus (Ancient Greek: Τάρταρος) is the deep abyss used as a dungeon of torment and suffering for the wicked, and as the prison for the Titans after their defeat. It appears in early Greek cosmology in Hesiod's Theogony as one of the earliest beings to exist alongside Chaos and Gaia. According to Plato's Gorgias (c. 400 BC), souls are judged there after death.

quiz_question: In Greek mythology, who could originally enter the Elysian Fields?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Elysium was initially separated from the Greek underworld and only mortals related to the gods and other heroes could be admitted past the river Styx. Later, the conception expanded to include those chosen by the gods, the righteous, and the heroic. Homer located the Elysian Fields on the western edge of the Earth by the stream of Oceanus.

quiz_question: Who appointed Hel as ruler of the Norse underworld realm?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hel is attested in the Poetic Edda and Prose Edda as the daughter of Loki. In the Prose Edda book Gylfaginning, Hel is described as having been appointed by the god Odin as ruler of a realm of the same name located in Niflheim. Her appearance is described as half blue and half flesh-colored.

quiz_question: What is the Norse name for the primordial void that existed before creation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ginnungagap (Old Norse: [ˈɡinːoŋɡɑˌɡɑp]; meaning 'gaping abyss' or 'yawning void') is the primordial, magical void mentioned in three poems from the Poetic Edda and the Gylfaginning, the Eddaic text recording Norse cosmogony. Wikidata confirms it is part of Norse cosmology and appears in the Völuspá.

quiz_question: The Chinese Great Flood myth is traditionally set during the reign of which legendary emperor?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Great Flood of Gun-Yu (Chinese: 鯀禹治水) was a major mythological flood in ancient China that continued for at least two generations, causing population displacements, storms, and famine. It is traditionally dated to about 2300–2200 BCE during the reign of Emperor Yao. Wikidata places it on the Yellow River around 3000 BCE.

quiz_question: The real-world worm lizard genus Amphisbaena is named after a creature from which mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amphisbaena is a real genus in the family Amphisbaenidae (worm lizards) with 102 species, native to South America and the Caribbean. They are legless, with ring-like scales, and highly reduced eyes. Wikidata confirms the genus was named after the mythological amphisbaena — a two-headed serpent of Greek mythology that could move in both directions.

quiz_question: What did Prometheus steal from the gods to give to humanity?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Prometheus was a Titan who defied the Olympian gods by giving fire to humanity in the form of technology, knowledge, and civilization. Zeus punished him by chaining him to a rock where an eagle ate his liver each day — which regenerated overnight — until Heracles eventually freed him.

quiz_question: What does the name 'Prometheus' literally mean in Greek?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Prometheus means 'forethought or foresight', while his brother Epimetheus means 'afterthought or hindsight'. Both are sons of the Titan Iapetus, and the brothers serve as archetypal representations of opposite human impulses. Epimetheus, who married Pandora, is portrayed as foolish compared to the clever Prometheus.

quiz_question: Which god forged Pandora, the first woman in Greek myth, on Zeus's instructions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pandora was created by Hephaestus on the instructions of Zeus. As Hesiod related it, each god cooperated by giving her unique gifts. Wikidata confirms her creators include Athena, Zeus, and Hephaestus. The Pandora myth addresses why there is evil in the world — she opened a jar (pithos, commonly misnamed 'box') releasing all evils of humanity.

quiz_question: In the oldest surviving Robin Hood ballads, what class did Robin Hood belong to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while modern retellings often depict Robin Hood as being of noble birth, in the oldest known versions he is a member of the yeoman class. The earliest known ballads featuring him date from the 15th century. His 'rob from the rich, give to the poor' stance and his setting in the Angevin era were developed in later centuries through retellings.

quiz_question: Why was Robin Hood's enormous lieutenant called 'Little' John?
correct_answer: Ironic reference to his huge size
distractors: He was the youngest Merry Man
He was the last to join
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Little John's name is an ironic reference to his giant frame — he is usually portrayed as a huge warrior, 7 feet (2.1 metres) tall and a master of the quarterstaff. He is one of the two oldest Merry Men alongside Much the Miller's Son, and serves as Robin Hood's chief lieutenant and second-in-command.

quiz_question: Which three celestial deities did the Titan Hyperion father with his sister Theia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in Greek mythology, Hyperion fathered Helios (Sun), Selene (Moon) and Eos (Dawn) with his sister, the Titaness Theia.

quiz_question: How many Muses did Mnemosyne bear with Zeus, making her the mother of all artistic inspiration?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mnemosyne was the goddess of memory and the mother of the nine Muses by Zeus. The name Mnemosyne is derived from the Greek word mnēmē meaning 'remembrance, memory'.

quiz_question: In Greek mythology, Tartarus served two purposes: as a dungeon of torment for the wicked and as a prison for what specific group?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tartarus is the deep abyss that is used as a dungeon of torment and suffering for the wicked and as the prison for the Titans.

quiz_question: According to Homer, where were the Elysian Fields located?
correct_answer: The western edge of the Earth by the stream of Oceanus
distractors: Beneath Mount Olympus in a hidden valley
On an island in the middle of the Aegean Sea
In the stars above the constellation Orion
Deep underground beneath the city of Athens
On the highest peak of the Caucasus mountains
At the center of the Earth near the Underworld
In the clouds above the realm of Zeus
On an island in the Mediterranean Sea east of Greece
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Elysian Fields were, according to Homer, located on the western edge of the Earth by the stream of Oceanus.

quiz_question: In Norse mythology, who appointed Hel as ruler of the underworld realm that bears her name?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the Prose Edda, Hel is described as having been appointed by the god Odin as ruler of a realm of the same name, located in Niflheim. She is referred to as a daughter of Loki.

quiz_question: What does the Old Norse name 'Ginnungagap' translate to in English?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ginnungagap (old Norse: 'gaping abyss', 'yawning void') is the primordial, magical void mentioned in three poems from the Poetic Edda and the Gylfaginning, the Eddaic text recording Norse cosmogony.

quiz_question: In Greek mythology, which god created Pandora on the instructions of Zeus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pandora was the first human woman created by Hephaestus on the instructions of Zeus. As Hesiod related it, each god cooperated by giving her unique gifts.

quiz_question: What does the name 'Epimetheus' literally mean in ancient Greek?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Epimetheus (Ancient Greek: Ἐπιμηθεύς, lit. 'afterthought') is the brother of Prometheus. While Prometheus ('forethought, or foresight') is portrayed as ingeniously clever, Epimetheus ('afterthought, or hindsight') is considered inept and foolish.

quiz_question: Creation myths are the most common form of myth globally. In what tradition do they primarily develop, explaining why they typically have multiple versions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, creation myths develop in oral traditions and therefore typically have multiple versions; found throughout human culture, they are the most common form of myth.

quiz_question: In the oldest known versions of the Robin Hood legend, what social class did Robin Hood belong to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the oldest known versions, Robin Hood is a member of the yeoman class — not of noble birth, as he is sometimes depicted in modern retellings.

quiz_question: What is ironic about the name 'Little John' in the Robin Hood legend?
correct_answer: He is actually portrayed as a giant, 7-foot-tall warrior
distractors: He is actually older than Robin Hood, not younger
He is actually the leader, not Robin Hood
He is actually the son of a nobleman, not a commoner
He is actually a terrible fighter who never wins
He is actually the tallest Merry Man — Little refers to his sword
He is actually cowardly despite his imposing name
He was actually named 'Great John' before joining the outlaws
He is actually the smallest member of the Merry Men
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Little John's name is an ironic reference to his giant frame, as he is usually portrayed in legend as a huge warrior — a 7-foot-tall (2.1-metre) master of the quarterstaff.

quiz_question: Who popularized the Paul Bunyan legend in 1916 through a promotional pamphlet for a lumber company?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the character of Paul Bunyan originated in the oral tradition of North American loggers and was later popularized by freelance writer William B. Laughead (1882–1958) in a 1916 promotional pamphlet for the Red River Lumber Company.

quiz_question: What are Baba Yaga's two most visually distinctive traits regarding her home and mode of transportation?
correct_answer: A hut with chicken legs and a flying wooden mortar
distractors: A flying broomstick and a house in a tree
A magic cauldron and an underground cave
A wolf companion and a house made of bones
A flying carpet and a tower of ice
A raven familiar and a floating island
A talking mirror and a castle of stone
An enchanted sleigh and a cave of crystal
A giant spider mount and a web-covered cottage
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Baba Yaga's distinctive traits are flying around in a wooden mortar, wielding a pestle, and dwelling deep in the forest in a hut with chicken legs.

quiz_question: How do selkies transform between their seal and human forms in folklore?
correct_answer: By removing or putting on their seal skin
distractors: By diving into enchanted seawater at midnight
By receiving a magical kiss from a human
By speaking a sacred incantation at the shore
By waiting for a full moon over the ocean
By shedding their outer scales in sunlight
By performing a ritual dance on the beach
By drinking from a magical freshwater spring
By transforming involuntarily every seven years
explanation: According to Wikipedia, selkies are mythological creatures that can shapeshift between seal and human forms by removing or putting on their seal skin.

quiz_question: On which river does archaeological evidence suggest the Great Flood of Gun-Yu myth was based?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, archaeological evidence of an outburst flood at Jishi Gorge on the Yellow River has been dated to about 1920 BCE and is suggested to have been the basis for the Great Flood of Gun-Yu myth.

quiz_question: Which Titan whose name means "afterthought" married Pandora in Greek myth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Epimetheus ("afterthought") is the brother of Prometheus ("forethought"), both sons of the Titan Iapetus. While Prometheus is legendary for cleverness, Epimetheus is considered inept and foolish. In some accounts, Epimetheus unleashes the unforeseen troubles in Pandoras box.

quiz_question: In Greek creation myths, which primordial entity came first and spawned both Night and Earth as children?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Chaos is the first entity in Greek cosmogony, with children including Erebos (Darkness), Nyx (Night), Uranus (Sky), Tartarus, Eros, and Gaia. Chaos is worshipped by both ancient Roman and Greek religions and is present in works about Hades.

quiz_question: What does Baba Yagas forest hut stand on, according to Slavic folklore?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Baba Yaga is a Slavic folkloric figure who dwells deep in the forest in a hut standing on chicken legs. She flies in a wooden mortar, uses a pestle to steer, and is associated with forest wildlife. She can be helpful or terrifying depending on the tale, playing both helper and villain.

quiz_question: Scholars call creation myths the most common form of myth worldwide. How do they typically develop?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, creation myths develop in oral traditions and therefore typically have multiple versions. They are found throughout human culture and are the most common form of myth, regarded as sacred accounts conveying profound truths. Historian of religion Mircea Eliade described the time they depict as in illo tempore, a sacred dim and nonspecific past.

quiz_question: Paul Bunyan was first popularized in print through a 1916 promotional pamphlet for which type of company?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Paul Bunyan originated in the oral tradition of North American loggers. Freelance writer William B. Laughead (1882-1958) popularized the character in a 1916 promotional pamphlet for the Red River Lumber Company. Paul is customarily accompanied by Babe the Blue Ox, his pet and working animal.

quiz_question: In Celtic and Norse folklore, how do selkies transform between seal and human form?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, selkies can shapeshift between seal and human forms by removing or putting on their seal skin. They are mainly associated with the Northern Isles of Scotland. A selkie can be trapped into marrying a human if that person steals and hides the seal skin, though such marriages are often unhappy as the selkie longs for the sea.

quiz_question: Almost every sizeable body of water in Scotland has a legend associated with which shape-shifting creature?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the kelpie (Scottish Gaelic: each-uisge) is a shape-shifting water spirit inhabiting lochs in Scottish folklore, usually appearing as a grey or white horse. Almost every sizeable body of water in Scotland has an associated kelpie story, but the most extensively reported is that of Loch Ness. Robert Burns referenced the kelpie in his 1786 poem Address to the Devil.

quiz_question: Friar Tuck is part of which famous band of folkloric outlaws in English legend?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Friar Tuck is one of the Merry Men, the band of heroic outlaws in the folklore of Robin Hood. Although a common character in the modern Robin Hood legend, Tuck does not appear in the earliest surviving Robin Hood ballads. He also appears in Sir Walter Scotts novel Ivanhoe.

quiz_question: The mythical two-headed serpent amphisbaena lent its name to a real genus of what kind of animal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amphisbaena is a real genus in the family Amphisbaenidae, commonly known as worm lizards. Currently 102 species are placed in this genus. They are legless, with ring-like scales and highly reduced eyes, and are subterranean predators native to South America and the Caribbean. The genus name was inspired by the mythological two-headed serpent.

quiz_question: What term describes any narrative or scientific model specifically about the origin of the universe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cosmogony (also spelled cosmogeny or cosmogenesis) is any model concerning the origin of the cosmos or universe. Unlike cosmology, which studies the universes structure, cosmogony specifically addresses its beginnings. Wikidata cites Islamic cosmogony as a model item for the concept.

quiz_question: Who killed and dismembered Osiris, forcing Isis to search Egypt to reassemble his body?
explanation: Wikipedia states 'When his brother Set cut him to pieces after killing him... Osiris's sister-wife, Isis, searched Egypt to find each part of Osiris. She collected all but one... She then wrapped his body up, enabling him to return to life.' Osiris became the Egyptian god of the afterlife and resurrection.

quiz_question: After the Osiris cult spread, which figures were identified with Osiris in death to gain eternal life?
explanation: Wikipedia states that 'the kings of Egypt were associated with Osiris in death — as Osiris rose from the dead, they would unite with him and inherit eternal life through imitative magic.' This connected the mythology of resurrection directly to royal power.

quiz_question: Compared to all other Egyptian gods, how was Isis's magical power described?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Isis's 'reputed magical power was greater than that of all other gods, and she was said to govern the natural world and wield power over fate itself.' She was initially a funerary and magical figure before becoming the most widely worshipped Egyptian goddess.

quiz_question: In the Egyptian afterlife, what was the soul's heart weighed against during the Weighing of the Heart?
explanation: Wikipedia states Anubis 'attended the weighing scale during the "Weighing of the Heart", in which it was determined whether a soul would be allowed to enter the realm of the dead.' If the heart was heavier than Maat's feather of truth, the soul was devoured by Ammit.

quiz_question: Which two animals were sacred to the Egyptian god Thoth, and appear in his depictions?
explanation: Wikipedia states Thoth 'was often depicted as a man with the head of an ibis or a baboon, animals sacred to him.' Thoth was the god of the Moon, wisdom, knowledge, writing, hieroglyphs, science, magic, art, and judgment.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian god of kingship were living pharaohs considered to be a manifestation of?
explanation: Wikipedia states Horus 'served many functions, most notably as the god of kingship, healing, protection, the sun, and the sky.' The living pharaoh was 'regarded as a manifestation of Horus in life and Osiris in death' — a brilliant theological system that legitimised royal power.

quiz_question: Which two Egyptian gods merged to create the powerful composite deity Amun-Ra?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'After the rebellion of Thebes against the Hyksos and with the rule of Ahmose I (16th century BC), Amun acquired national importance, expressed in his fusion with the Sun god, Ra, as Amun-Ra.' This composite deity became 'champion of the poor or troubled and central to personal piety.'

quiz_question: What is the literal Nahuatl translation of Quetzalcoatl's name?
explanation: Wikipedia states Quetzalcoatl's name in Nahuatl means 'Feathered Serpent'. He was associated with 'wind, Venus, Sun, merchants, arts, crafts, knowledge, and learning' and was 'the patron god of the Aztec priesthood'.

quiz_question: How did the Irish hero Cú Chulainn earn his famous name as a child?
correct_answer: By killing Culann's guard dog and offering to replace it
distractors: By defeating a rival warrior in battle
By taming a wild wolf in the forest
By retrieving a stolen hound for the king
By surviving a hunt with a pack of hounds
By training war hounds for King Conchobar
By healing a mortally wounded guard dog
By outrunning the king's fastest hound
By wrestling a giant wolfhound into submission
explanation: Wikipedia states Cú Chulainn 'gained his better-known name as a child, after killing Culann's fierce guard dog in self-defence and offering to take its place until a replacement could be reared, hence he became the "Hound (cú) of Culann".'

quiz_question: In world literature, which ancient Mesopotamian figure is considered the oldest literary representation of the 'wild man' archetype?
explanation: Wikipedia states Enkidu 'is the oldest literary representation of the wild man, a recurrent motif in artistic representations in Mesopotamia and in Ancient Near East literature.' He was created as a rival to Gilgamesh but became his closest friend.

quiz_question: In Scottish folklore, what is a kelpie?
explanation: Wikipedia states a kelpie is 'a mythical shape-shifting spirit inhabiting lochs in Scottish folklore' and 'is usually described as a grey or white horse-like creature, able to adopt human form.' The most extensively reported kelpie legend is associated with Loch Ness.

quiz_question: What animal form did the Egyptian goddess Bastet originally represent before she became associated with cats?
explanation: Wikipedia states Bastet 'was worshipped in Bubastis in Lower Egypt, originally as a lioness goddess, a role shared by other deities such as Sekhmet. Eventually Bastet and Sekhmet were characterized as two aspects of the same goddess, with Sekhmet representing the powerful warrior... and Bastet, who increasingly was depicted as a cat, representing a gentler aspect.'

quiz_question: Which supreme Hindu god is almost never worshipped despite being one of the Trimurti?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, despite being one of the three supreme deities alongside Vishnu and Shiva, Brahma lost his significance by the 7th century CE and is rarely worshipped as a primary deity. The most famous temple dedicated to him is in Pushkar, Rajasthan.

quiz_question: What do Brahma's four heads each symbolize in Hindu mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Brahma is commonly depicted with four heads, each pointing to a cardinal direction. Each head represents one of the four Vedas — the ancient Hindu sacred scriptures.

quiz_question: How did Brahma create his children, according to Hindu scripture?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the scriptures say Brahma created his offspring — called Manasaputra, meaning 'mind-born sons' — from his mind rather than through physical means. This includes sages like Narada, Daksha, and Vasishtha.

quiz_question: Which goddess is considered the divine ancestor of Japan's Imperial family?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amaterasu is portrayed in Japan's earliest texts — the Kojiki (c. 712 CE) and Nihon Shoki (720 CE) — as the mythical ancestress of the Imperial House of Japan via her grandson Ninigi.

quiz_question: What collective name unites Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi, and Susanoo in Shinto?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi, and Susanoo rank as the 'Three Precious Children' (三貴子, mihashira no uzu no miko / sankishi), the three most important offspring of Izanagi — the creator god.

quiz_question: In which ancient Japanese text does Amaterasu first appear in writing?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amaterasu is portrayed in Japan's earliest literary texts — the Kojiki (c. 712 CE) and the Nihon Shoki (720 CE). The Kojiki is Japan's oldest surviving chronicle.

quiz_question: What monstrous serpent did the Japanese storm god Susanoo famously battle?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Susanoo is portrayed as a heroic figure who killed a monstrous serpent. Yamata no Orochi is listed as his enemy in Wikidata claims. Susanoo's occupation is recorded as 'dragonslayer' in Wikidata.

quiz_question: What makes Susanoo unusual among Japanese gods in how he is portrayed?
distractors: He appears in no ancient texts
He was invented in the modern era
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Susanoo is described as 'a multifaceted deity with contradictory characteristics (both good and bad)' — portrayed as a wild storm god, a heroic dragon-slayer, and a local harvest deity depending on the story.

quiz_question: How was the Monkey King Sun Wukong born in Journey to the West?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the 16th-century novel Journey to the West, Sun Wukong is described as 'a monkey born from a stone who acquires supernatural powers through Taoist practices.'

quiz_question: How many unique forms can Sun Wukong access using his Earthly Transformations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Sun Wukong acquires the 72 Earthly Transformations, which allow him to access 72 unique powers, including the ability to transform into animals and objects.

quiz_question: Who imprisoned Sun Wukong under a mountain after his rebellion against heaven?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after Sun Wukong rebelled against heaven, he was imprisoned under a mountain by the Buddha. He remained trapped for five hundred years before being released to accompany the monk Tang Sanzang on a pilgrimage.

quiz_question: What natural force is the Asian Dragon King primarily known for controlling?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Dragon King can manipulate and control the weather, move seasons, and bring rainfall with his divine power. He is regarded as the dispenser of rain and the divine ruler of seas, rivers, and all water bodies.

quiz_question: Where does the Dragon King hold his royal court in Asian mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Dragon King is described as having his own underwater palace and a royal court system from which he rules over all bodies of water in Asian mythology.

quiz_question: What special power does Erlang Shen's third eye grant him in Chinese mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Erlang Shen is commonly depicted as a young man with a third, truth-seeing eye in the middle of his forehead, wielding a three-pronged spear, and being accompanied by his loyal hunting dog, Xiaotian Quan.

quiz_question: Who is Erlang Shen most commonly believed to be the deification of?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Erlang Shen is most commonly believed to be the deification of Li Erlang, the second son of Li Bing, a hydraulic engineer of the Qin dynasty (221–206 BC). Later stories alternatively identify him as Yang Jian, the Jade Emperor's nephew.

quiz_question: What is Erlang Shen's relationship to the Jade Emperor in Journey to the West?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the Ming-era novel Journey to the West, Erlang Shen is the second son of a mortal and the Jade Emperor's sister Yunhua, making him the Jade Emperor's nephew. He is known as the greatest warrior god of heaven in these legends.

quiz_question: What does the Aztec deity name 'Quetzalcoatl' literally mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Quetzalcoatl is a Nahuatl name meaning 'Feathered Serpent'. He was one of the most important Aztec deities, associated with wind, Venus, the sun, merchants, arts, crafts, and knowledge — an unusually broad divine portfolio for a single deity.

quiz_question: Which dog-headed Aztec deity was Quetzalcoatl's sibling and guide of the dead?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Quetzalcoatl's siblings included Xolotl, Xipe Totec, Tezcatlipoca, and Huitzilopochtli. Xolotl, the dog-headed deity, served as psychopomp — escorting souls of the dead to the underworld.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian goddess resurrected her murdered husband and conceived a divine heir?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Isis was first mentioned in the Old Kingdom (c. 2686–2181 BCE) as one of the main characters of the Osiris myth, in which she resurrects her slain brother and husband Osiris and produces and protects his heir Horus. She was considered the divine mother of the pharaoh.

quiz_question: Isis worship eventually spread beyond Egypt to encompass which ancient civilization?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Isis was a major goddess in ancient Egyptian religion whose worship spread throughout the Greco-Roman world. Her cult became one of the most widespread mystery religions of the ancient Mediterranean.

quiz_question: How did the Irish hero Sétanta earn the name Cú Chulainn?
correct_answer: Offered to replace Culann's guard dog
distractors: Tamed a wild wolf at court
Won a hound as a battle prize
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the hero was born Sétanta and gained the name Cú Chulainn ('Hound of Culann') as a child after killing Culann's fierce guard dog in self-defence and offering to take its place until a replacement could be reared.

quiz_question: At what age did Cú Chulainn defend Ulster single-handedly against Connacht?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, it was prophesied that Cú Chulainn's great deeds would give him everlasting fame, but that his life would be short. At the age of seventeen he defended Ulster single-handedly against the armies of Connacht.

quiz_question: What did Anubis weigh a dead person's heart against in the Egyptian judgment of souls?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, one of Anubis's prominent roles was attending the weighing scale during the 'Weighing of the Heart', in which it was determined whether a soul would be admitted to the afterlife. The heart was weighed against a feather of Ma'at, goddess of truth and justice.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian dynasty first depicted Anubis as a protector of graves?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Anubis was depicted as a protector of graves as early as the First Dynasty (c. 3100–2890 BC). By the Middle Kingdom (c. 2055–1650 BC), he was replaced by Osiris in his role as lord of the underworld, but retained his roles as embalmer and soul guide.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian god was the living pharaoh considered to be an incarnation of?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Horus served as the god of kingship, healing, protection, the sun, and the sky. He was most often depicted as a falcon. The living pharaoh was identified with Horus while the deceased pharaoh was identified with Osiris, Horus's father.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian god created human bodies using a potter's wheel and clay?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Khnum was one of the earliest-known Egyptian deities, originally associated with the Nile cataract. He eventually became known as the creator of human bodies and the life force ka. Using a potter's wheel and clay, he fashioned human bodies and placed them within their mothers' wombs.

quiz_question: Which ancient Mesopotamian figure is the oldest known literary 'wild man' archetype?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Enkidu was a legendary figure in ancient Mesopotamian mythology and wartime comrade of Gilgamesh. He is the oldest literary representation of the wild man, a recurrent motif in Mesopotamian and Ancient Near East art and literature, appearing in the Akkadian Epic of Gilgamesh written during the 2nd millennium BC.

quiz_question: In what year does the Annales Cambriae first mention Mordred alongside King Arthur?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the earliest known mention of a possibly historical Medraut is in the Welsh chronicle Annales Cambriae, where he and Arthur are ambiguously associated with the Battle of Camlann in a brief entry for the year 537. Medraut was actually regarded positively in early Welsh tradition — the 'traitor' character was a later medieval invention.

quiz_question: How was Pegasus born in Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus was foaled by the Gorgon Medusa and sired by Poseidon. When Perseus decapitated Medusa, Pegasus and his brother Chrysaor both sprang from her blood — two mythological beings born simultaneously from a single violent act.

quiz_question: What sacred spring did Pegasus create on Mount Helicon?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus is the creator of Hippocrene, the fountain on Mount Helicon. This spring was sacred to the Muses and was said to be the source of poetic inspiration — struck into existence by the hooves of a flying horse.

quiz_question: Which hero rode Pegasus to slay the Chimera in Greek myth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pegasus was captured by the Greek hero Bellerophon near the fountain Peirene, with help from Athena and Poseidon. Bellerophon rode Pegasus to defeat the Chimera — but his hubris led him to try reaching Olympus itself, for which he fell from Pegasus's back.

quiz_question: What role did Pegasus serve for Zeus on Mount Olympus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after his ascent to heaven, Pegasus obeyed Zeus, who instructed him to bring lightning and thunder from Olympus. Some tales have Zeus bring Pegasus to Olympus specifically to carry his thunderbolts — a remarkable role for a winged horse.

quiz_question: What was Cerberus's main purpose guarding the underworld's gates?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cerberus is a multi-headed dog that guards the gates of the underworld to prevent the dead from leaving — not to keep the living from entering. This subtle distinction reverses most people's assumption about what the monster was actually for.

quiz_question: Which of Heracles' twelve labours involved capturing Cerberus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cerberus is primarily known for his capture by Heracles, described as the last of Heracles' twelve labours. Descending into Hades itself to capture its guardian was the ultimate challenge — the grand finale of the legendary series of tasks.

quiz_question: What monstrous parents did Cerberus share with the Hydra and Chimera?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Cerberus was the offspring of the monsters Echidna and Typhon. His siblings include the Hydra, Chimera, the Nemean lion, and Orthrus — making Echidna and Typhon the most prolific monster-parents in Greek mythology.

quiz_question: What political event elevated the god Amun to supreme deity of Egypt?
distractors: Egypt unified Upper and Lower kingdoms
The Great Pyramid was dedicated to him
The Ptolemaic dynasty rose to power
The Nile flood destroyed rival temples
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after the rebellion of Thebes against the Hyksos and with the rule of Ahmose I (16th century BC), Amun acquired national importance, expressed in his fusion with the sun god Ra as Amun-Ra. His rise was political — Thebes's local god was elevated to king of the gods as a national reward.

quiz_question: What is a werewolf described as in folklore?
correct_answer: Human who shifts into wolf form
distractors: A wolf-human hybrid born that way
A cursed wolf given human thoughts
A ghost that appears as a wolf
A wolf god of old Europe
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a werewolf, in folklore, is a mythical being described as a person who shapeshifts into a wolf-like creature, either purposely or after being placed under a curse or affliction. Werewolf legends appear widely across European cultures.

quiz_question: How does a legend differ from a myth in folklore?
correct_answer: Legends feature human actions believed historical
distractors: Myths are always shorter than legends
Legends can only be told orally
Myths involve heroes while legends use gods
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a legend is a genre of folklore that consists of a narrative featuring human actions, believed or perceived to be historical. This distinguishes it from myths, which typically focus on gods, the cosmos, and origins of the world rather than individual human deeds.

quiz_question: How did the Irish hero Sétanta earn his famous warrior name?
correct_answer: Offered to replace Culann's guard dog
distractors: Tamed a wild wolf at court
Won a hound as a battle prize
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the hero was born Sétanta and gained the name Cú Chulainn ('Hound of Culann') as a child after killing Culann's fierce guard dog in self-defence and offering to take its place until a replacement could be reared.

quiz_question: Which Egyptian god created human bodies using clay on a potter's wheel?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Khnum was one of the earliest-known Egyptian deities, originally associated with the Nile cataract. He became known as the creator of human bodies and the life force ka. Using a potter's wheel and clay, he fashioned human bodies and placed them within their mothers' wombs.

quiz_question: In what year does the Annales Cambriae first place Mordred alongside King Arthur?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the earliest known mention of a possibly historical Medraut is in the Welsh chronicle Annales Cambriae, where he and Arthur are ambiguously associated with the Battle of Camlann in a brief entry for the year 537. Medraut was actually regarded positively in early Welsh tradition — the 'traitor' characterization came centuries later.

quiz_question: In the Hebrew Bible, which creature embodies chaos and threatens to eat the damned?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Leviathan is a giant sea serpent referenced throughout the Hebrew Bible — in Psalms, Job, Isaiah, and the Book of Enoch — as a metaphor for chaos and powerful enemies like Babylon. It is often depicted threatening to eat the damned and is ultimately annihilated at the end of time.

quiz_question: Which deadly sin did Christian theologians associate the sea-monster Leviathan with?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Christian theologians identified Leviathan with the demon of envy — one of the seven deadly sins. This tradition linked the chaos-serpent of the Hebrew Bible to a specific moral failing in Christian demonology.

quiz_question: Which philosopher used Leviathan as a metaphor for the all-powerful state?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hobbes transformed Leviathan from a biblical chaos-monster into a metaphor for the omnipotent state — a power that perpetuates itself by conditioning each new generation through education. This concept of eternal state power feeding on the citizens it continuously produces is central to Hobbes's political philosophy.

quiz_question: Which Sumerian sea goddess is compared to Leviathan in comparative mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, scholars of comparative mythology have long noted parallels between Leviathan and the primeval Sumerian sea goddess Tiamat (defeated by Marduk), as well as dragon and world-serpent narratives like Indra slaying Vritra or Thor battling Jörmungandr — suggesting a shared ancient archetype of a chaos-serpent defeated by a sky god.

quiz_question: Which biblical book introduces Behemoth as a chaos-monster made by God at creation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Behemoth appears in the biblical Book of Job as a form of the primeval chaos-monster created by God at the beginning of creation. It stands alongside Leviathan as one of God's most awe-inspiring original creatures, presented to humble Job.

quiz_question: What modern meaning has the biblical word 'behemoth' taken on in everyday language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name Behemoth originated as a Hebrew word (בְּהֵמוֹת) for the biblical chaos-monster in the Book of Job. Metaphorically, it has entered common usage to describe any extremely large or powerful entity — a linguistic legacy of its biblical origins.

quiz_question: Beyond being a chaos-monster, what occupational role does Behemoth hold in religious demonology?
explanation: According to Wikidata claims for Behemoth, the creature is listed with the occupation of 'demon' in addition to being a mythical creature and biblical character. This reflects traditions in demonology that placed Behemoth among the great demonic powers alongside Leviathan.

quiz_question: What is the Ancient Greek word for a female nature-spirit in Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Ancient Greek word for these female nature-spirits is νύμφη (nymphe). In Greek mythology nymphs were divine female spirits associated with different elements of nature — waters, trees, mountains, and the sea each had their own type of nymph.

quiz_question: What is a gargoyle, at its most fundamental definition?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a gargoyle is a grotesque statue. The term specifically applies to these monstrous stone figures found on buildings — particularly Gothic cathedrals — where they have historically served as both architectural decoration and functional waterspouts that drain rainwater away from walls.

quiz_question: What specific tree did the Greek word 'Dryad' originally refer to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Greek word 'Drys' (δρῦς) means 'oak', so Dryads were originally oak tree spirits specifically. Today the term is used broadly for any tree nymph, but the oak connection is the true etymological origin.

quiz_question: Dryads are a subclass of which broader category of Greek supernatural beings?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Dryads are classified as a subclass of Greek nymphs, tree spirits, and Epigean beings. They were worshipped in Ancient Greece as feminine nature deities tied specifically to trees.

quiz_question: What was Orthrus — the two-headed Greek monster — guarding when Heracles killed him?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Orthrus was a two-headed dog who guarded the cattle belonging to the giant Geryon. He was killed by the hero Heracles, according to the mythographer Apollodorus.

quiz_question: Which monstrous couple in Greek myth were the parents of Orthrus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Orthrus was born from Echidna (the mother of monsters) and Typhon (the most fearsome monster in Greek myth). His siblings included Cerberus and Hydra, and he fathered the Nemean Lion and the Sphinx.

quiz_question: Which two famous monsters was Orthrus said to have fathered?
explanation: According to Wikidata claims, Orthrus fathered both the Nemean Lion (whose hide was invulnerable) and the Sphinx (who devoured those who couldn't answer her riddle). Both became iconic challenges for Greek heroes.

quiz_question: How many real lizard species belong to the genus named after the mythical Amphisbaena?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Amphisbaena is a real genus of legless worm lizards in the family Amphisbaenidae with 102 species. The genus was named after the mythical two-headed serpent from Greek mythology. These real lizards are subterranean and native to South America and the Caribbean.

quiz_question: What two physical features define a Harpy in Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a Harpy is a female monster in Greek mythology. Wikidata identifies them as creatures with a woman's head and a bird's body — winged spirits of sudden destruction who snatched victims away.

quiz_question: What did the ancient Egyptian goddess Bastet protect?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Bastet is an ancient Egyptian goddess, depicted as a domestic cat or as a woman with a cat head. She was worshipped as protector of the home, a goddess of domesticity, women's secrets, cats, and fertility. Cats were so sacred in ancient Egypt that killing one, even accidentally, was a capital offense.

quiz_question: According to Herodotus, approximately how many people attended the great festival of Bastet at Bubastis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the ancient Greek historian Herodotus described the annual festival at Bubastis (Tell Basta) in honor of Bastet as the most joyous and well-attended festival in all of Egypt, with over 700,000 pilgrims arriving by boat, singing, dancing, and making offerings.

quiz_question: Which ancient Egyptian god was credited with inventing hieroglyphs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Thoth is an ancient Egyptian deity associated with the Moon, wisdom, writing, hieroglyphs, science, magic, art, and judgment. He was credited with the invention of writing, hieroglyphs, and all branches of human knowledge. He was typically depicted as a man with the head of an ibis bird.

quiz_question: What role did Thoth play in the Egyptian afterlife judgment ceremony?
correct_answer: Recording the verdict of the heart weighing
distractors: Guarding the gates of the afterlife
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the Egyptian Book of the Dead, Thoth served as the divine recorder in the Hall of Two Truths (the hall of judgment). When the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at, Thoth recorded the outcome, which determined whether the soul would enter the afterlife or be devoured by Ammit.

quiz_question: What natural feature was said to be created by Sekhmet's breath?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Sekhmet is a warrior goddess as well as goddess of healing depicted as a lioness. The hot winds of the desert were said to be her breath, and she was said to have created the desert with that same breath. Despite being a goddess of destruction, she was also the patron of physicians and healers.

quiz_question: How was Sekhmet stopped from destroying all of humanity in Egyptian myth?
correct_answer: She got drunk on red beer she thought was blood
distractors: Thoth tricked her with a riddle
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in Egyptian mythology Ra sent Sekhmet to punish humankind for their transgressions, but she went on an uncontrollable rampage. To stop her, the gods flooded a field with red-dyed beer, which Sekhmet mistook for blood. She drank it all, became intoxicated, and forgot her murderous mission, thus saving humanity.

quiz_question: How many lines long is the Old English epic poem Beowulf?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Beowulf is an Old English epic poem consisting of 3,182 alliterative lines. It is the longest surviving Old English poem and is generally considered the most important work of Old English literature. It was composed between the 8th and 11th centuries.

quiz_question: What kills Beowulf in the Old English epic?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the epic poem Beowulf, the hero battles three monsters: first Grendel (who has been terrorizing the mead hall Heorot), then Grendel's vengeful mother, and finally — decades later as an aging king — a fire-breathing dragon. Beowulf defeats the dragon but dies from the wounds inflicted during the fight.

quiz_question: Who is the monster Grendel said to be descended from in Beowulf?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the poem Beowulf, Grendel is referred to as a descendant of Cain, the Biblical figure who murdered his brother Abel. This lineage explains both Grendel's monstrous nature and his rage — as a descendant of the first murderer, he is cursed and exiled from human society, filled with envy at the joy he witnesses in the mead hall Heorot.

quiz_question: What was the symbol of Ma'at that was weighed against the hearts of the dead?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ma'at was the ancient Egyptian concept and goddess of truth, justice, order, and cosmic balance. She was depicted wearing an ostrich feather on her head, and this feather was the symbol of cosmic truth. In the afterlife judgment, the deceased's heart was weighed against this feather — if the heart was heavy with sin, the soul was destroyed.

quiz_question: What does the hieroglyphic headdress of Nephthys symbolize?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Nephthys (also spelled Nebet-Het) is an ancient Egyptian deity. Her name literally means 'Lady of the House' or 'Mistress of the House' — written in hieroglyphics as a combination of symbols for house (Het) and lady (Nebet). She was a funerary goddess who, alongside her sister Isis, guarded and mourned the dead.

quiz_question: What battle did Huitzilopochtli fight every day in Aztec mythology?
correct_answer: Against the night sky to make the sun rise
distractors: Against the moon to protect the earth
Against rival gods to rule the sky
Against death to preserve human life
Against the underworld to protect crops
Against wind gods to calm storms
Against sea monsters to protect ships
Against the stars to prevent eternal night
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Huitzilopochtli was the Aztec deity of the sun and war, and the patron god of the city of Tenochtitlan. Each dawn was conceived as Huitzilopochtli's victory over the forces of night. Human sacrifices were believed necessary to give him the strength to fight this daily battle and keep the sun rising.

quiz_question: How many dog heads did the sea monster Scylla have, according to the Suda?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Byzantine encyclopedia Suda describes Scylla as having the appearance of a beautiful woman up to the eyes, with six dog heads on each side, and a serpent body below — making her a horrifying hybrid creature who lurked on one side of a deadly strait.

quiz_question: What modern idiom derives from the Greek myth of Scylla and Charybdis?
correct_answer: Between a rock and a hard place
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the idiom 'between Scylla and Charybdis' has come to mean being forced to choose between two similarly undesirable or risky outcomes — an ancient myth that gave us a phrase still used in everyday language today.

quiz_question: What was Scylla before she became a monster, according to later Greek myths?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while Homer's Odyssey presents Scylla as simply a monstrous threat, later myths added an origin story in which she was originally a beautiful nymph who was transformed into the hideous monster. This narrative pattern — beauty turned to horror — is common in Greek mythology.

quiz_question: Who were the mythological parents of Charybdis, the whirlpool monster?
explanation: According to Wikidata claims, Charybdis was fathered by Poseidon (god of the sea) and born of Gaia (the primordial earth). This unusual parentage — the sea god and the earth itself — gave her the terrifying power to swallow the sea three times daily.

quiz_question: In which real-world strait did ancient Greeks believe Charybdis and Scylla lurked?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greek mythical chroniclers and historians located Charybdis and Scylla in the Strait of Messina — the narrow passage between Sicily and Calabria in southern Italy. The real treacherous currents there likely inspired the myth.

quiz_question: What do Japanese Baku creatures eat, according to folklore?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Baku (獏 or 貘) are Japanese supernatural beings that devour nightmares. Legend says they were created from the spare pieces left over when the gods finished creating all other animals — making them uniquely patchwork creatures in Japanese folklore.

quiz_question: From which Chinese mythical creature did the Japanese Baku originate?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Baku originate from the Chinese Mo — a mythological creature that Japan adapted and developed into the distinctly nightmare-devouring supernatural being known in Japanese folklore today.

quiz_question: Which three groups did Gaia conceive with Uranus in Greek mythology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gaia is the personification of Earth in Greek mythology. With Uranus (Sky), she conceived the Titans (who became parents of the Olympian gods), the Cyclopes, and the Giants. She also bore Pontus (Sea) and had more children with him.

quiz_question: What was the Roman name for the Greek earth goddess Gaia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gaia's equivalent in the Roman pantheon was Terra. Wikidata confirms she was 'said to be the same as Terra' — a common pattern in Greco-Roman mythology where deities from each tradition were matched to counterparts.

quiz_question: Which Greek primordial god unusually had no surviving religious cult?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, no cult addressed directly to Uranus survived into classical times, and he does not appear among the usual themes of Greek painted pottery — making him an unusual primordial deity who influenced cosmology but received no worship.

quiz_question: Where was the Greek sky god Uranus imprisoned after being overthrown?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Uranus's place of detention is listed as Tartarus — the deep abyss used as a dungeon of torment. His son Cronus overthrew him and imprisoned him there, beginning the reign of the Titans before Zeus eventually overthrew Cronus in turn.

quiz_question: Among the Titans of Greek mythology, what rank did Cronus hold?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cronus was the leader and youngest of the Titans, the children of Gaia and Uranus. He overthrew his father Uranus and ruled during the mythological Golden Age until he was overthrown by his own son Zeus and imprisoned in Tartarus.

quiz_question: What was the iconic symbol of the Greek Titan Cronus, linked to his harvest origins?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and confirmed by Wikidata (iconographic symbol: sickle), Cronus was usually depicted with a harpe, scythe, or sickle. He was likely originally a harvest god, and Athens held a festival called Kronia on the twelfth day of Hekatombaion to celebrate the harvest in his honor.

quiz_question: Which day of the week is named after the Roman equivalent of the Greek Titan Cronus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Cronus was identified in classical antiquity with the Roman deity Saturn. Wikidata confirms 'said to be the same as Saturn.' The planet Saturn and the word Saturday both derive from Saturn's name — a Greek Titan living on in our weekly calendar!

quiz_question: Which three celestial beings did the Titan Hyperion father with his sister Theia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Titan Hyperion fathered Helios (Sun), Selene (Moon) and Eos (Dawn) with his sister Theia. Together these three siblings personified the three great daily celestial transitions — day, night, and the moments between.

quiz_question: Which Greek mythological figure was sometimes considered the same as his son Helios?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hyperion was, along with his son Helios, a personification of the sun — with the two sometimes identified as the same deity. This dual identification is common in Greek mythology where earlier Titan versions of roles were absorbed into their Olympian successors.

quiz_question: Which Titaness was the mother of all nine Muses by Zeus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mnemosyne was the goddess of memory and mother of the nine Muses by Zeus. The name Mnemosyne is derived from the Greek word mnēmē ('remembrance, memory') — the same root that gives us English words like 'mnemonic.'

quiz_question: Which modern English word comes directly from the Greek goddess Mnemosyne's name root?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name Mnemosyne is derived from the Greek word mnēmē ('remembrance, memory'). This root directly gives us the English word 'mnemonic' — a memory aid device. So every time you use a mnemonic, you're using a tool named after this ancient Titaness.

quiz_question: Tlaloc was the Aztec god of which natural force?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tlaloc is the god of rain in Aztec religion. He is one of the oldest and most universal figures of worship in ancient Mexico — although the name Tlaloc is specifically Nahuatl, worship of a storm deity with similar attributes dates back to at least the civilization at Teotihuacan, thousands of years earlier.

quiz_question: Which Yoruba orisha governs love, fertility, and fresh water?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Oshun (also spelled Osun) is the orisha of love, intimacy, beauty, prosperity, and fertility in the Yoruba religion. She is described as one of the most popular of the 401 orishas in the Yoruba pantheon and is queen consort to Shango. She is honored annually at the Osun-Osogbo Festival in Osogbo, Nigeria, each August.
